meta.title: Mercedes-Benz M111 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz M111 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes M111 is a 1.8–2.3L four-cylinder petrol — robust and simple, but timing chain and head gasket issues come with age. Backed by UK specialist consensus [MB-INTERVIEW].

rows.value: Confidence: 88% confidence — based on specialist feedback and long‑term owner data across multiple platforms.

rows.metric: Most failures are cheap, well‑understood repairs (gaskets, sensors, chains)

rows.value: Parts cheap and widely available from multiple UK suppliers

rows.metric: Reconditioned cost is 30–44% of lowest host vehicle value (~£1,500)

rows.value: Sum of the five rows above

rows.metric: One-line verdict: The M111 is a tough, simple petrol four — age‑related chain wear and head gasket leaks are common — buy on condition and service history, not just mileage.
rows.value: Best for: Classic Mercedes buyers wanting a dependable, easy‑to‑work‑on engine.

rows.metric: Avoid if: You're not prepared for routine gasket and chain maintenance on older cars.

The M111 is one of Mercedes‑Benz’s most enduring four‑cylinder petrol engines, produced from 1992 to 2006 in displacements of 1.8, 2.0, 2.2, and 2.3 litres. It was available in naturally aspirated and supercharged (Kompressor) forms, powering everything from the C‑Class and E‑Class to the SLK, CLK, and even the V‑Class.
* Simple, robust architecture — iron block, aluminium head
rows.value: * Non‑interference — timing chain failure (if it occurs) often does not destroy the engine (check specific variant)

rows.metric: * Excellent parts availability — both genuine and aftermarket
rows.value: * Easy to work on — few electronics, ample access in most models

rows.metric: * Long‑standing reputation — many examples exceed 200,000 miles
* Timing chain stretch — can cause rattling and potentially jump timing if neglected
rows.value: * Head gasket failure — oil/coolant leaks, particularly around 100,000–150,000 miles

rows.metric: * Oil leaks — valve cover gasket, rear main seal
rows.value: * Ignition system — distributor or coil pack failure on earlier models

rows.metric: * Supercharger clutch (Kompressor versions) — can fail, reducing power
rows.value: * MAF sensor and throttle body — can cause rough idle

rows.metric: Despite its age, the M111 remains a highly regarded engine for its durability and ease of maintenance. Routine chain inspection and gasket replacement every 100,000 miles will keep it running well into high mileage.

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an M111 typically takes 6–10 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [ESTIMATED].

cta.label: → Compare M111 engine prices from UK specialists

items.question: How much does an M111 engine replacement cost?
items.answer: A reconditioned M111 typically costs £800–£1,800 supply only [ESTIMATED], varying by Kompressor or naturally aspirated version. Fitting adds £390–£950 depending on labour rates and vehicle access.

items.question: Is the M111 a reliable engine?
items.answer: Yes — the M111 rates 4.0★ out of 5 based on specialist feedback and long‑term owner data [MB-INTERVIEW]. Its simple design and durable components make it one of the more dependable older Mercedes engines.

items.question: What is the most common M111 failure?
items.answer: Timing chain stretch is the most commonly reported issue, typically appearing from 80,000–120,000 miles. Head gasket leaks and oil leaks from the valve cover are also frequent on higher‑mileage units.

items.question: Which Mercedes models use the M111?
items.answer: The M111 powers C‑Class (W202/W203), E‑Class (W210), SLK (R170), CLK (W208/W209), A‑Class (W168), V‑Class (W638), and some G‑Class and Sprinter variants — covering a broad range from the mid‑1990s to mid‑2000s.

items.question: How long does the M111 last?
items.answer: With routine timing chain and gasket maintenance, the M111 routinely exceeds 200,000 miles. Many specialists report examples at 250,000+ with original internals [MB-INTERVIEW].
